front: Event: its history is chiefly recorded in Jean Froissart's Chronicles, considered the best primary source for its events
Event: prompt-worthy alternate names for its phases include the Edwardian War/Phase, the Caroline War, and the Lancastrian War
Event: a later, unrelated "Second Hundred Years' War" nickname is applied to the Anglo-French conflicts of 1689-1815, which Britain helped fund with its excise tax
Event: grew out of a succession dispute between Philip VI and Edward III after the senior line of the Capetian dynasty failed to produce a male heir
work: Causes & Outbreak: Succession Dispute and the Battle of Sluys

front: Figure: Edward III's claim to the French throne came through his mother, Isabella of France, a former lover of Roger Mortimer
work: Causes & Outbreak: Succession Dispute and the Battle of Sluys

front: Event: confusion over Salic law fueled the succession dispute between Edward III and Philip VI
work: Causes & Outbreak: Succession Dispute and the Battle of Sluys

front: Event: Philip VI's confiscation of English-held Aquitaine, gained by England when Eleanor of Aquitaine married Henry II, made Edward III a vassal and helped spark the war
work: Causes & Outbreak: Succession Dispute and the Battle of Sluys

front: Battle: the war formally began with an English naval victory at this battle, fought near the Scheldt estuary off the coast of Flanders
work: Causes & Outbreak: Succession Dispute and the Battle of Sluys

front: Battle: Edward the Black Prince achieved a major victory here, alongside Poitiers
Battle: the French hired Genoese crossbowmen to fight against Edward III here
Battle: the blind King John of Bohemia died here, and the English victor subsequently adopted his crest of three ostrich feathers
Battle: Edward the Black Prince captured King John II "the Good" of France here; John yielded himself to the banished French knight Denis of Morbeke, who was fighting for England
work: Battle of Poitiers (1356) & the Treaty of Bretigny

front: Treaty: John II's capture and ransom here ended the war's "Edwardian phase"
work: Battle of Poitiers (1356) & the Treaty of Bretigny

front: Treaty: ransomed John II for three million ecus, 40 hostages, and territories including Guyenne and Gascony
work: Battle of Poitiers (1356) & the Treaty of Bretigny

front: Treaty: hostage Louis I, Duke of Anjou, dishonorably escaped captivity under this treaty, prompting John II to surrender himself as a replacement prisoner
work: Battle of Poitiers (1356) & the Treaty of Bretigny

front: Event: the resulting political instability from John II's capture at Poitiers helped spark the Jacquerie peasant revolt
work: Battle of Poitiers (1356) & the Treaty of Bretigny

front: Event: led by Guillaume Cale, nicknamed "Jack Goodfellow" (or "Jacques Bonhomme") by chronicler Jean Froissart
Event: Charles the Bad of Navarre tricked and imprisoned Cale before crushing the revolt at the Battle of Mello
Event: its name derives from peasants' padded tunics ("jacques")
Event: its motivations paralleled those of the English Peasants' Revolt of 1381, led by Wat Tyler
Event: a succession dispute in the House of Dreux, following Duke John III's death, pitted the Montfort family against the Blois family for control of Brittany
Battle: English forces backing the Montforts, aided by Sir John Chandos, won this decisive battle, after which Brittany then swore homage to France anyway
Event: featured the Combat of the Thirty, a celebrated feat of medieval chivalry in which Robert Bemborough was killed
back: War of the Breton Succession (Combat of the Thirty)

front: Culture: fast, devastating cavalry raids on the countryside, a tactic earlier used by the Spanish, carried out by Edward III's troops and, most famously, by Edward the Black Prince

Culture: demobilized mercenary "free companies" (routiers) that turned to brigandry included the pillaging "late-comers" and "Flayers of Dead Men," led by Rodrigo de Villandrando, the "Emperor of Pillagers"
Figure: a siege expert nicknamed the Archpriest personally confessed to the Pope after extorting villages subject to the Queen of Naples
Figure: Italian condottiere John Hawkwood led many English and French war veterans in the mercenary White Company
Work: knight Geoffroi de Charny's participation in the war informed this influential treatise
Figure: later notorious serial killer Gilles de Rais fought in the war, at one point helping relieve a siege of an Armagnac-aligned city
Event: after fighting together in the war, knight Jacques le Gris was killed by his former comrade Jean de Carrouges in a high-profile judicial duel
Battle: fought on Saint Crispin's Day; commemorated by Shakespeare's Henry V, including the king's speech beginning "we happy few, we band of brothers"
Battle: a cavalry charge across muddy terrain catastrophically slowed the French forces under commander Charles d'Albret, who was killed in the battle
Battle: English and Welsh longbowmen, used by up to 80% of English troops and often wielding bows made of yew wood, decimated the French cavalry here
Battle: longbow-wielding archers destroyed the army of the absent King Charles VI here
Treaty: Henry V's victory at Agincourt was followed by his marriage to Catherine of Valois and this 1420 treaty establishing his heirs' claim to the French throne
Figure: Joan of Arc, a peasant girl who later helped crown Charles VII at Reims, led the French army in lifting this English siege in 1429
work: Joan of Arc & the Siege of Orleans (1429)

front: Figure: Joan of Arc was later captured by Burgundian forces, handed to the English, and burned at the stake for the heresy of cross-dressing
work: Joan of Arc & the Siege of Orleans (1429)

front: Event: Joan of Arc's intervention after the Siege of Orleans turned the tide of the war against the English in its later phases
work: Joan of Arc & the Siege of Orleans (1429)

front: Ruler: appointed Bertrand du Guesclin as constable and won a naval victory at the Battle of La Rochelle with Castilian help
work: Valois Kings: Charles V, Charles VI & Isabeau of Bavaria

front: Ruler: marauding ecorcheurs harassed French peasants during this king's reign
work: Valois Kings: Charles V, Charles VI & Isabeau of Bavaria

front: Ruler: believed he was made of glass; a masquerade ball thrown by his queen, Isabeau of Bavaria, ended when a torch set costumed dancers on fire (the Bal des Ardents)
work: Valois Kings: Charles V, Charles VI & Isabeau of Bavaria

front: Ruler: Isabeau of Bavaria's reputation suffered due to polemics exchanged during the Burgundian-Armagnac civil war, which broke out amid her husband Charles VI's incapacity
work: Valois Kings: Charles V, Charles VI & Isabeau of Bavaria

front: Ruler: was nicknamed "the Victorious" after his armies captured Bordeaux and defeated the English at Castillon, ending the war
work: End of the War: Castillon & the Treaty of Picquigny

front: Battle: English commander John Talbot, previously captured at Patay, was killed at this 1453 battle
work: End of the War: Castillon & the Treaty of Picquigny

front: Battle: Jean Bureau set up a fortified camp with a ditch and cannon here, reducing the effectiveness of the longbow and ending English dominance
work: End of the War: Castillon & the Treaty of Picquigny

front: Event: the English defeat at Castillon unofficially ended the war, leaving England with only the Pale of Calais as a continental holding
work: End of the War: Castillon & the Treaty of Picquigny

front: Treaty: signed in 1475 when Louis XI "the Spider King" defeated Charles the Bold of Burgundy, formally ending the Hundred Years' War with England
work: End of the War: Castillon & the Treaty of Picquigny
